Failure Cause Resolution Cannot export transactional table ACID tables cannot be exported directly Enable acid.downgradeAcid: true or use HYBRID Insufficient disk space Export directory runs out of space Use a different export path with more space IMPORT fails: Incompatible table format Version mismatch between source/target Hive Check both Hive versions — may need SQL instead Permission denied on export path Migration user can't write to staging directory Grant write permission on HDFS staging path

docs.title: HYBRID Strategy
docs.text:
HYBRID automatically chooses between SQL and EXPORT_IMPORT based on table size and partition count. Failures can be from either sub-strategy.

Key configuration thresholds: 

hybrid:
  sqlPartitionLimit: 500        # Tables with fewer partitions → SQL
  exportImportPartitionLimit: 1000  # Tables exceeding this → fail or skip

docs.title: STORAGE_MIGRATION
docs.text:
Moves data between storage locations (e.g., HDFS → S3) without re-ingesting.

Failure Cause Resolution Source path not accessible HMS-Mirror can't read source HDFS Check HCFS configuration in connection Target path not writable No permission on target storage Check cloud credentials or HDFS ACLs Namespace mismatch Wrong HCFS namespace configured Verify Default FS in connection

docs.title: ACID / Transactional Table Issues
docs.text:
ACID tables require special handling:

Error: Cannot EXPORT transactional table with ACID properties

Option 1 — Downgrade ACID to external: 

acid:
  downgradeAcid: true

Option 2 — Use EXPORT_IMPORT with ACID support enabled (requires compatible Hive versions)

Option 3 — Use SQL strategy (resource-intensive for large ACID tables)

docs.title: Partition-Heavy Tables
docs.text:
Tables with many partitions are a common performance and failure source:

Error: Too many dynamic partitions: 50000 (maximum: 1000)

Resolution: 

optimizations:
  overrides:
    - key: hive.exec.max.dynamic.partitions
      rightValue: "100000"
    - key: hive.exec.dynamic.partition.mode
      rightValue: nonstrict

If tables have tens of thousands of partitions, EXPORT_IMPORT or STORAGE_MIGRATION is preferred over SQL.

docs.title: Partial Success Jobs
docs.text:
If a job succeeds for some tables and fails for others, this is normal — HMS-Mirror continues past individual table failures and reports the summary. The customer can:

  1. Fix the failing tables manually
  2. Re-run the job with a dataset scoped to only the failed tables (use include regex)
  3. Accept the partial migration and address remaining tables in a follow-up job

docs.title: Report Interpretation
docs.text: After a migration job runs, HMS-Mirror generates a set of report files. This page explains what each file contains and how to interpret the results.

docs.title: Report Location
docs.text:
~/.hms-mirror/reports/<job-id>/

List reports: 

ls ~/.hms-mirror/reports/

docs.title: Report Files
docs.text: File Contents Audience conversion-result.yaml Full machine-readable result with per-table status L2/L3 debugging report.html Human-readable HTML summary Customer / PM left-execute.sql SQL that was executed against the SOURCE cluster L2 debugging right-execute.sql SQL that was executed against the TARGET cluster L2 debugging left-dbo.sql DDL for source database Reference right-dbo.sql DDL for target database Reference errors.txt Tables that failed with reasons L1/L2 first look

docs.title: Reading conversion-result.yaml
docs.text:
The YAML file has a hierarchical structure:

conversionResult:
  databases:
    sales_db:
      tables:
        orders:
          overall: SUCCESS       # or FAILURE, PARTIAL, SKIP
          actions:
            - type: CREATE_TABLE
              status: SUCCESS
              sql: "CREATE TABLE ..."
            - type: INSERT_DATA
              status: FAILURE
              error: "OutOfMemoryError"
          source:
            ddl: "CREATE TABLE orders ..."
          target:
            ddl: "CREATE TABLE orders ..."

docs.title: Status Values
docs.text: Status Meaning SUCCESS Completed without errors FAILURE Did not complete — error field has details PARTIAL Some actions succeeded, others failed SKIP Table was intentionally skipped (excluded by dataset filter or strategy) ISSUE Completed but with warnings

docs.title: All Tables SUCCESS
docs.text:
The migration is complete. Tables have been created on the target cluster.

For data-moving strategies (SQL, EXPORT_IMPORT, HYBRID): Data has been copied.

For SCHEMA_ONLY: Only table definitions were copied — data is NOT in the target tables.

docs.title: Some Tables FAILURE
docs.text:
This is expected and normal for large migrations. Reasons vary:

  1. Permission issues — specific databases/tables the migration user can't access
  2. Incompatible types — column types that don't exist on the target
  3. ACID tables — transactional tables that need special handling
  4. Very large tables — hit partition or resource limits

The customer should review errors.txt to understand each failure and decide which tables need manual intervention.

docs.title: All Tables SKIP
docs.text: Usually means: - The dataset filter was too restrictive - The database doesn't exist on the source - All tables were already migrated and overwrite is disabled

docs.title: PARTIAL Success
docs.text: Some operations on the table succeeded (e.g., CREATE TABLE worked) but data movement failed. The table exists on the target but may be empty or incomplete.

docs.title: left-execute.sql / right-execute.sql
docs.text:
These show the actual SQL statements that HMS-Mirror executed. Useful for: - Understanding what the tool did vs. what the customer expected - Reproducing a failure manually: copy the failing statement and run it in beeline - Verifying that the correct DDL was generated

-- Example right-execute.sql snippet
USE target_db;
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS orders (
  order_id BIGINT,
  customer_id BIGINT,
  amount DECIMAL(10,2),
  order_date DATE
)
STORED AS ORC
LOCATION '/warehouse/tablespace/managed/hive/target_db.db/orders';

docs.title: Understanding SQL Transformation
docs.text:
HMS-Mirror often transforms DDL between source and target. Common transformations:

Transformation Why Storage path rewritten Different HDFS namespace between clusters MANAGED → EXTERNAL ACID downgrade or strategy choice PURGE added CDP external tables need this for DROP behavior SerDe removed Source SerDe not available on target

docs.title: Common Interpretation Questions
docs.text:
Q: "Why didn't my views get migrated?"

Views are not migrated by default with some strategies. Check the ConfigLite migrate.views setting.

Q: "The table was created but it's empty."

This is expected for SCHEMA_ONLY or LINKED strategy. If the customer expected data, they chose the wrong strategy.

Q: "The migration succeeded but data looks different."

Check the SQL files — the migration may have done a type conversion, a CTAS (CREATE TABLE AS SELECT), or a partition restructure. Review right-execute.sql.

Q: "Some tables succeeded but not all. Do we need to start over?"

No — run a second job scoped to only the failed tables using an include regex that matches the failed table names. HMS-Mirror supports incremental/partial migrations.

docs.title: L3 Backline Support
docs.text:
Audience: Senior engineers with deep Hadoop ecosystem knowledge, JVM internals familiarity, and direct access to customer environments when needed.

Prerequisites: Complete familiarity with L1 and L2 documentation. L3 work assumes L2 has already diagnosed the problem category and ruled out configuration errors.

Your job: Solve problems that require deep technical knowledge: database-level operations, security infrastructure, JVM tuning, incident response, and engineering escalation packaging.

docs.title: What L3 Handles
docs.text:
• RocksDB diagnostics, recovery, and backup/restore
  • TLS/SSL certificate chain issues and replacement
  • Kerberos deep debugging (KDC, keytab, realm configuration)
  • JVM performance profiling and heap analysis
  • Security incident response (account compromise, audit)
  • Encryption keystore recovery procedures
  • API-level debugging with authentication tokens
  • Engineering escalation package preparation
  • Production data recovery coordination

docs.title: What L3 Does NOT Handle
docs.text:
• Direct database manipulation without explicit procedure
  • Code changes (escalate to Engineering)
  • Customer cluster administration (guide customer, don't do it for them)

docs.title: L3 Diagnostic Environment
docs.text:
Before starting any L3 work, establish a safe working context:

# Know exactly where the working directory is
echo "Working dir: ${HMS_MIRROR_WORKING_DIR:-$HOME/.hms-mirror}"

# Check disk space before any operations
df -h ~/.hms-mirror/

# Get current server status
curl -s http://localhost:8090/hms-mirror/api/v1/system/health | python3 -m json.tool

# Backup the data directory before any changes
tar -czf hms-mirror-data-backup-$(date +%Y%m%d-%H%M).tar.gz ~/.hms-mirror/

Always Backup First

Before any RocksDB repair, keystore operation, or configuration change at L3, create a backup. Many L3 operations are irreversible.

docs.title: Pages in This Section
docs.text: Page When to Use RocksDB Diagnostics Database corruption, startup failure, data recovery TLS/SSL Deep Dive Certificate errors, HTTPS setup, trust chain Kerberos Deep Dive Kerberos auth failures beyond basic kinit Performance & Memory OOM, slow jobs, JVM heap analysis Security Incidents Unauthorized access, lockout investigation Keystore Recovery Encryption keystore lost or corrupted API-Level Debugging Direct API calls with curl for diagnosis Engineering Escalation Package Preparing a complete bug report for engineering

docs.title: API-Level Debugging
docs.text: When the UI gives insufficient information, direct API calls with curl can provide more detail. This page covers the full authentication flow and key diagnostic endpoints.

docs.title: Step 1: Get an Access Token
docs.text:
# Login to get tokens
RESPONSE=$(curl -s -X POST \
  http://localhost:8090/hms-mirror/api/v1/auth/login \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "usernameOrEmail": "admin",
    "password": "adminpassword"
  }')

echo $RESPONSE | python3 -m json.tool

# Extract access token
TOKEN=$(echo $RESPONSE | python3 -c "import sys,json; print(json.load(sys.stdin)['accessToken'])")
REFRESH=$(echo $RESPONSE | python3 -c "import sys,json; print(json.load(sys.stdin)['refreshToken'])")

echo "Access token: $TOKEN"

docs.title: Step 2: Use the Token
docs.text:
# All subsequent requests use the Bearer token
curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  http://localhost:8090/hms-mirror/api/v1/system/health | python3 -m json.tool

docs.title: Step 3: Refresh When Token Expires
docs.text:
Access tokens expire in 15 minutes. Use the refresh token:

NEW_TOKEN=$(curl -s -X POST \
  http://localhost:8090/hms-mirror/api/v1/auth/refresh \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d "{\"refreshToken\": \"$REFRESH\"}" | \
  python3 -c "import sys,json; print(json.load(sys.stdin)['accessToken'])")

docs.title: System Status Endpoints
docs.text:
# Overall health
curl -s http://localhost:8090/hms-mirror/api/v1/system/health | python3 -m json.tool

# Authenticated status endpoints
curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  http://localhost:8090/hms-mirror/api/v1/status | python3 -m json.tool

# JVM status (heap, threads)
curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  http://localhost:8090/hms-mirror/api/v1/status/jvm | python3 -m json.tool

# RocksDB status
curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  http://localhost:8090/hms-mirror/api/v1/status/rocksdb | python3 -m json.tool

docs.title: User Management via API
docs.text:
# List all users
curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  http://localhost:8090/hms-mirror/api/v1/users | python3 -m json.tool

# Get a specific user
curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  http://localhost:8090/hms-mirror/api/v1/users/<userId> | python3 -m json.tool

# Create a user (admin only)
curl -s -X POST \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "username": "newuser",
    "email": "newuser@example.com",
    "password": "Secure1!",
    "admin": false
  }' \
  http://localhost:8090/hms-mirror/api/v1/users | python3 -m json.tool

# Unlock a user
curl -s -X PUT \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  http://localhost:8090/hms-mirror/api/v1/users/<userId>/unlock

# Reset password (admin)
curl -s -X PUT \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"newPassword": "TempPass1!"}' \
  http://localhost:8090/hms-mirror/api/v1/users/<userId>/admin-reset-password

docs.title: Connection Management via API
docs.text:
# List all connections
curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  http://localhost:8090/hms-mirror/api/v1/connections | python3 -m json.tool

# Test a specific connection
curl -s -X POST \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  http://localhost:8090/hms-mirror/api/v1/connections/<connectionId>/test | python3 -m json.tool

docs.title: Job Management via API
docs.text:
# List all jobs
curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  http://localhost:8090/hms-mirror/api/v1/jobs | python3 -m json.tool

# Get a specific job with its result
curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  http://localhost:8090/hms-mirror/api/v1/jobs/<jobId> | python3 -m json.tool

# Get job run history
curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  http://localhost:8090/hms-mirror/api/v1/jobs/<jobId>/history | python3 -m json.tool

# Download job report
curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  -o report.zip \
  http://localhost:8090/hms-mirror/api/v1/jobs/<jobId>/report

docs.title: Debugging HTTP Errors
docs.text:
For 4xx/5xx responses, enable verbose curl output:

# -v shows full request/response headers
# -S shows errors
curl -vs \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  http://localhost:8090/hms-mirror/api/v1/jobs 2>&1 | head -40

docs.title: Common HTTP Error Codes
docs.text: Code Meaning Likely Cause 400 Bad Request Invalid request body or parameters 401 Unauthorized Missing or expired token 403 Forbidden Token valid but lacks required role 404 Not Found Wrong URL or resource doesn't exist 409 Conflict Duplicate resource (e.g., username already exists) 500 Internal Server Error Bug or unexpected server condition — check logs

docs.title: SSE Endpoints (LLM Features)
docs.text:
For SSE endpoints that require the ?token= query parameter (cannot set Bearer header via EventSource):

# Download/watch LLM model progress via SSE (if LLM feature enabled)
TOKEN_ENCODED=$(python3 -c "import urllib.parse; print(urllib.parse.quote('$TOKEN'))")

curl -N -H "Accept: text/event-stream" \
  "http://localhost:8090/hms-mirror/api/v1/llm/models/pull?model=llama2&token=$TOKEN"

docs.title: Engineering Escalation Package
docs.text: When L3 cannot resolve an issue and suspects a code defect, an incomplete escalation package will delay engineering investigation. This page defines the complete package.

docs.title: When to Escalate to Engineering
docs.text: Trigger Required Evidence Reproducible bug (not config/env issue) Exact steps to reproduce Unexpected API response (not 4xx from bad input) curl example with exact request/response RocksDB corruption with no environmental cause Data directory backup + full log Performance regression between versions Before/after comparison Security vulnerability suspected Private channel — do NOT file a public ticket Data loss or corruption in migration results Job ID, report files, SQL executed

docs.title: 1. Bug Report Title Format
docs.text:
[BUG] [v<version>] <Concise one-line description>

Examples:
[BUG] [v4.1.5] EXPORT_IMPORT jobs fail silently for ACID tables with no error logged
[BUG] [v4.1.5] createUser API returns 500 when email contains a + character
[BUG] [v4.1.5] RocksDB corruption after ungraceful shutdown on Docker restart

docs.title: 2. Environment
docs.text:
HMS-Mirror version: 4.1.5.8  (exact — from JAR filename or API)
Java version: 17.0.9+9 (OpenJDK)
OS: RHEL 8.9
Deployment: Cloudera Manager CSD, parcel 4.1.5.8-el8
Working dir: /var/lib/hms-mirror/.hms-mirror

docs.title: 3. Exact Reproduction Steps
docs.text:
Number every step. Include exact commands, exact UI actions:

1. Log in as admin (local auth)
2. Navigate to Connections → Create New
3. Enter: Name=TestConn, Platform=HDP, JDBC=jdbc:hive2://host:10000/default
4. Click "Test Connection"
5. Observe: 500 Internal Server Error
   Expected: "Connection successful" or a meaningful error

Frequency: 100% reproducible with this exact URI format

docs.title: 4. Observed vs. Expected
docs.text: Description Observed HTTP 500 with no error message in response body Expected HTTP 400 with a human-readable error explaining why the URI is invalid, or HTTP 200 if the connection should succeed

docs.title: 5. Relevant Log Excerpt
docs.text:
Only the relevant lines — not the entire log. For exceptions, include the full stack trace:

2026-02-26 10:15:33.421 ERROR c.c.u.h.m.service.ConnectionService - Unexpected error testing connection: TestConn
java.lang.NullPointerException: Cannot invoke "String.startsWith(String)" because "jdbcUrl" is null
    at com.cloudera.utils.hms.mirror.service.impl.ConnectionServiceImpl.validateUri(ConnectionServiceImpl.java:142)
    at com.cloudera.utils.hms.mirror.service.impl.ConnectionServiceImpl.testConnection(ConnectionServiceImpl.java:87)
    ... (truncated)

docs.title: 6. API Request/Response (if applicable)
docs.text:
# Request
curl -s -X POST \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer <token>" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"name":"TestConn","platform":"HDP","jdbcUrl":"jdbc:hive2://host:10000/default"}' \
  http://localhost:8090/hms-mirror/api/v1/connections/test

# Response (500)
{"timestamp":"2026-02-26T10:15:33.421+00:00","status":500,"error":"Internal Server Error","path":"/hms-mirror/api/v1/connections/test"}

docs.title: 7. Attached Files
docs.text: File Required? Why Last 500 lines of log Mandatory Context and stack trace Thread dump For hang/deadlock bugs Current thread state Heap dump For OOM bugs Memory analysis Job report files For migration bugs Execution details RocksDB backup For data/startup bugs State at time of failure Sanitized web.properties For config-dependent bugs Env reproduction

docs.title: Security Vulnerability Report
docs.text:
For suspected security vulnerabilities, use a private channel — do NOT file a public GitHub issue.

Include in the private report: 1. CVE category (if known): OWASP Top 10 category 2. Description: what can an attacker do? 3. Prerequisites: what access does an attacker need? 4. Proof of concept: minimal steps to demonstrate (not to exploit) 5. Severity assessment: CVSS score estimate if possible 6. Suggested remediation: your recommendation

docs.title: Patch Verification
docs.text:
After engineering releases a fix, L3 verifies:

  1. The exact steps that previously reproduced the bug no longer reproduce it
  2. No regression in adjacent functionality
  3. The fix is confirmed on the customer's exact environment (version, OS, deployment mode)
  4. Document: "Verified fixed in version X.Y.Z.B on [date] by [L3 engineer]"

docs.title: Kerberos Deep Dive
docs.text: Kerberos is a common source of obscure failures. This page goes beyond the basic kinit fix to cover realm issues, keytab management, and HMS-Mirror-specific Kerberos configuration.

docs.title: Kerberos Concepts Refresher
docs.text:
User / Service          KDC (Key Distribution Center)
    │                           │
    ├──── AS-REQ (kinit) ───────►│
    │◄─── AS-REP (TGT) ──────────┤
    │                            │
    ├──── TGS-REQ (use TGT) ────►│
    │◄─── TGS-REP (service ticket)│
    │                            │
    ├──── AP-REQ (to Hive) ─────────────────►│ HiveServer2
    │◄─── AP-REP (authenticated) ────────────┤

HMS-Mirror acts as a Kerberos client (equivalent to a user) connecting to HiveServer2 as a Kerberos service.

docs.title: Kerberos Configuration Locations
docs.text:
# Kerberos client config
cat /etc/krb5.conf

# List current tickets
klist -e

# Check keytab contents
klist -kt /path/to/service.keytab

docs.title: HMS-Mirror Kerberos Configuration
docs.text:
In the Connection JDBC URI: 

jdbc:hive2://hiveserver2.example.com:10000/default;principal=hive/hiveserver2.example.com@REALM

For Hadoop Kerberos config, the server needs access to: - krb5.conf (usually /etc/krb5.conf) - A valid ticket or keytab

In web.properties or startup JVM args: 

-Djava.security.krb5.conf=/etc/krb5.conf
-Djavax.security.auth.useSubjectCredsOnly=false

docs.title: Common Kerberos Errors and Root Causes
"GSS initiate failed"
docs.text:
javax.security.sasl.SaslException: GSS initiate failed [Caused by GSSException:
No valid credentials provided (Mechanism level: Failed to find any Kerberos tgt)]

Root cause: No Kerberos TGT (Ticket Granting Ticket) available.

Fix: 

# Check if ticket exists and is valid
klist

# Get a new ticket
kinit user@REALM.EXAMPLE.COM
# or with keytab (non-interactive)
kinit -kt /path/to/service.keytab user@REALM.EXAMPLE.COM

docs.title: "Clock skew too great"
docs.text:
KrbException: Clock skew too great (37) - PROCESS_TGS

Root cause: Time difference between the HMS-Mirror host and the KDC is greater than 5 minutes (Kerberos default tolerance).

Fix: 

# Check current time on HMS-Mirror host
date -u

# Sync with NTP
sudo ntpdate -u <ntp-server>
# or on systemd systems
sudo timedatectl set-ntp true
sudo chronyc makestep

docs.title: "Server not found in Kerberos database"
docs.text:
GSSException: No valid credentials provided
KrbException: Server not found in Kerberos database (7)

Root cause: The service principal in the JDBC URI doesn't match what's registered in the KDC.

Diagnosis: 

# Check what principal HiveServer2 is using
# (on the HiveServer2 host, or ask the Hadoop admin)
grep "principal" /etc/hive/conf/hive-site.xml

# Verify the principal in the JDBC URI matches exactly
# jdbc:hive2://host:10000/default;principal=hive/host.example.com@REALM

docs.title: "KDC unreachable"
docs.text:
KrbException: Cannot locate KDC for realm REALM.EXAMPLE.COM

Root cause: Cannot reach port 88 (UDP/TCP) of the KDC.

Diagnosis: 

# Test KDC connectivity (UDP)
nc -zuv kdc.realm.example.com 88

# Check krb5.conf for KDC address
grep kdc /etc/krb5.conf

# Attempt kinit with verbose output
kinit -V user@REALM

docs.title: "Integrity check on decrypted field failed"
docs.text:
KrbException: Integrity check on decrypted field failed (31)

Root cause: Password/keytab mismatch, or the principal's password was changed after the keytab was generated.

Fix: Regenerate the keytab from the KDC (requires Hadoop admin involvement).

docs.title: Keytab Management
docs.text: Keytabs are files containing pre-encrypted Kerberos credentials — they allow non-interactive kinit.

docs.title: Verify a Keytab
docs.text:
# List principals in a keytab
klist -kt /path/to/service.keytab

# Test authentication with keytab
kinit -kt /path/to/service.keytab principal@REALM

# Confirm the resulting ticket
klist

docs.title: Configure HMS-Mirror to Use a Keytab
docs.text:
For production deployments, configure a keytab so the server can authenticate automatically:

In web.properties: 

hms-mirror.kerberos.keytab=/path/to/hms-mirror.keytab
hms-mirror.kerberos.principal=hms-mirror/host.example.com@REALM

Or pass as JVM args: 

-Dhms-mirror.kerberos.keytab=/path/to/service.keytab
-Dhms-mirror.kerberos.principal=hms-mirror/host@REALM

docs.title: Multi-Realm Kerberos
docs.text:
If source and target clusters are in different Kerberos realms, cross-realm trust must be configured at the KDC level. This is outside HMS-Mirror's control — escalate to the customer's Kerberos/security administrator if cross-realm trust is not set up.

Symptom: Authentication works for source cluster but not target (or vice versa).

Verification: 

# Verify realm in target JDBC URI principal
# The principal realm must match the realm the KDC trusts

# List realm-specific configuration in krb5.conf
grep -A 10 "\[realms\]" /etc/krb5.conf

docs.title: Keystore Recovery
docs.text:
HMS-Mirror uses two keystores:

Keystore Purpose Location Encryption keystore Encrypts sensitive data at rest in RocksDB (passwords, credentials) ~/.hms-mirror/security/hms-mirror.p12 TLS keystore HTTPS certificate and key for the web server ~/.hms-mirror/certificates/

This page covers the encryption keystore. For TLS, see TLS/SSL Deep Dive.

docs.title: Understanding the Encryption Keystore
docs.text:
The encryption keystore stores the master encryption keys used to encrypt: - Connection passwords stored in RocksDB - Other sensitive configuration values

Configuration in web.properties: 

hms-mirror.keystore.path=~/.hms-mirror/security/hms-mirror.p12
hms-mirror.keystore.password=changeit

Keystore Loss = Encrypted Data Loss

If the keystore is lost and no backup exists, any encrypted values in RocksDB (connection passwords, etc.) cannot be decrypted. The data structure is intact but encrypted fields are unrecoverable without the key.

docs.title: Keystore File Verification
docs.text:
# Check the keystore file exists and is not empty
ls -la ~/.hms-mirror/security/

# Verify the keystore is readable with the configured password
keytool -list \
  -keystore ~/.hms-mirror/security/hms-mirror.p12 \
  -storetype PKCS12 \
  -storepass changeit

# Expected output shows the key entries

docs.title: Backup the Keystore
docs.text:
# Create a backup with timestamp
cp ~/.hms-mirror/security/hms-mirror.p12 \
   ~/.hms-mirror/security/hms-mirror.p12.backup.$(date +%Y%m%d)

# Store this backup in a SECURE location off the server
# (encrypted storage, secrets vault, secure S3 bucket with server-side encryption)

Backup schedule recommendation: After every configuration change that involves passwords, and at minimum weekly.

docs.title: Scenario: Keystore File Missing
docs.text:
If the keystore file is missing:

ls: cannot access ~/.hms-mirror/security/hms-mirror.p12: No such file or directory

And you have a backup:

cp /path/to/backup/hms-mirror.p12 ~/.hms-mirror/security/hms-mirror.p12
chmod 600 ~/.hms-mirror/security/hms-mirror.p12
/path/to/hmsm-server start

And you do NOT have a backup:

The encrypted fields in RocksDB are permanently unrecoverable. The procedure is:

  1. Stop the server
  2. Accept that connection passwords will need to be re-entered
  3. Start the server — HMS-Mirror will auto-generate a new keystore
  4. Re-configure all connection passwords in the UI
  5. Re-test all connections

docs.title: Scenario: Wrong Keystore Password
docs.text:
If the password in web.properties doesn't match the keystore file:

WARN  Keystore: Failed to load keystore — UnrecoverableKeyException

Fix:

  1. If you know the correct password, update web.properties: 

    hms-mirror.keystore.password=correct-password

  2. If the password is unknown and no backup exists — same outcome as lost keystore above.

docs.title: Scenario: Keystore Corruption
docs.text:
java.security.KeyStoreException: Failed to load keystore data

Try restoring from backup. If no backup:

  1. Stop the server
  2. Move the corrupt keystore: 
    mv ~/.hms-mirror/security/hms-mirror.p12 \
   ~/.hms-mirror/security/hms-mirror.p12.corrupt.$(date +%Y%m%d)
  3. Start the server — a new keystore will be generated
  4. Re-enter all connection passwords

docs.title: Changing the Keystore Password
docs.text:
# Change the keystore password
keytool -storepasswd \
  -keystore ~/.hms-mirror/security/hms-mirror.p12 \
  -storetype PKCS12 \
  -storepass oldpassword \
  -new newpassword

# Update web.properties to match
sed -i 's/hms-mirror.keystore.password=.*/hms-mirror.keystore.password=newpassword/' \
  ~/.hms-mirror/settings/web.properties

# Restart
/path/to/hmsm-server restart

docs.title: Best Practices for Key Management
docs.text:
1. Back up the keystore immediately after first startup — before configuring any connections
  2. Store the keystore backup and password separately — in different secure systems
  3. Test restoration — periodically verify the backup can be restored
  4. Use a secrets manager in production (HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, etc.) to store the keystore password instead of plain text in web.properties

docs.title: Identifying Performance Problems
docs.text:
Before tuning, determine what kind of performance issue it is:

Symptom Likely Cause OutOfMemoryError: Java heap space Heap too small for workload OutOfMemoryError: GC overhead limit exceeded Heap fragmentation / too many short-lived objects OutOfMemoryError: Metaspace Too many dynamically-loaded classes Migration runs for hours, then times out Individual query timeout, not heap Server gradually slows down over days Memory leak — escalate to Engineering Server slow from first startup Undersized JVM or hardware

docs.title: Check Current Heap Settings
docs.text:
# Find the running process and its JVM args
ps aux | grep java | grep hmsm

# Or check the startup script
cat /path/to/hmsm-server | grep -A 5 "JVM_OPTS\|Xmx\|Xms"

docs.title: Increase Heap Size
docs.text:
Set via environment variable before starting:

export JVM_OPTS="-Xmx8g -Xms4g"
/path/to/hmsm-server start

Or via web.properties: 

server.jvm.memory=-Xmx8g -Xms4g

Sizing Guidelines:

Dataset Size Recommended Heap < 100 tables 2GB (-Xmx2g) 100 - 1000 tables 4GB (-Xmx4g) 1000 - 5000 tables 8GB (-Xmx8g) 5000+ tables 16GB+ (-Xmx16g)

Note

These are starting points. ACID tables and partition-heavy tables require significantly more memory.

docs.title: GC Tuning
docs.text:
For G1GC (default in Java 17+):

export JVM_OPTS="-Xmx8g -Xms4g \
  -XX:+UseG1GC \
  -XX:G1HeapRegionSize=16m \
  -XX:MaxGCPauseMillis=200 \
  -XX:G1NewSizePercent=20 \
  -XX:G1MaxNewSizePercent=40 \
  -XX:+GCTimeLimit=20 \
  -XX:GCHeapFreeLimit=10"

docs.title: Thread Dump Analysis
docs.text:
When the server is slow or hung, get a thread dump:

PID=$(cat ~/.hms-mirror/hms-mirror.pid)

# Method 1: Signal (appears in server log)
kill -3 $PID

# Method 2: jstack (writes to file)
jstack $PID > /tmp/hms-mirror-threads-$(date +%H%M%S).txt

# Analyze the dump
# Look for:
# - Threads in "BLOCKED" state (lock contention)
# - Threads all waiting on the same monitor
# - Thread stuck in a JDBC call (database hang)
grep "BLOCKED\|waiting to lock\|waiting on" /tmp/hms-mirror-threads-*.txt | head -20

docs.title: Heap Dump Analysis
docs.text:
For OOM errors, get a heap dump:

PID=$(cat ~/.hms-mirror/hms-mirror.pid)

# Live heap dump (server keeps running)
jmap -dump:format=b,live,file=/tmp/hms-mirror-heap-$(date +%H%M%S).hprof $PID

# Add to startup args to auto-dump on OOM
export JVM_OPTS="-Xmx8g -XX:+HeapDumpOnOutOfMemoryError -XX:HeapDumpPath=/tmp/hms-mirror-oom.hprof"

Analyze with Eclipse Memory Analyzer (MAT) or VisualVM: - Look for the largest retained objects (usually ConversionResult or DBMirror lists) - Check for unexpected data duplication

docs.title: Query Timeout Configuration
docs.text:
For SQL strategy, queries have timeouts. If tables are large, the query may time out before completing:

# In ConfigLite
transfer:
  queryTimeout: 3600   # seconds (1 hour)

docs.title: Parallelism
docs.text:
HMS-Mirror can process multiple tables in parallel. Check the parallelism setting:

transfer:
  concurrentMetadataTaskLimit: 10   # parallel metadata tasks
  concurrentDataTaskLimit: 5        # parallel data tasks

Increasing parallelism speeds up total job time but increases load on the clusters. Too much parallelism can cause resource contention on the Hive clusters.

docs.title: Strategy Choice
docs.text: If SQL strategy is slow, consider switching to EXPORT_IMPORT: - SQL: copies data via INSERT INTO SELECT * — fully in-memory, fast for small tables - EXPORT_IMPORT: uses native Hive EXPORT/IMPORT — disk-based, better for large tables

docs.title: Disk I/O
docs.text:
RocksDB is I/O intensive. If the machine running HMS-Mirror has slow storage:

# Check disk I/O while server is running
iostat -x 5 3

# Check RocksDB compaction frequency (high I/O indicates compaction)
grep "Compaction\|compaction" ~/.hms-mirror/logs/hms-mirror.log | tail -20

Moving ~/.hms-mirror/data/ to an SSD can significantly improve startup time and query performance.

docs.title: System Resource Snapshot for Escalation
docs.text:
# Collect all resource data at once
{
  echo "=== DATE ==="
  date

  echo "=== JVM ARGS ==="
  ps aux | grep java | grep hmsm

  echo "=== MEMORY ==="
  free -h

  echo "=== DISK ==="
  df -h ~/.hms-mirror/

  echo "=== TOP PROCESSES ==="
  top -b -n 1 | head -20

  echo "=== JAVA HEAP (if jstat available) ==="
  PID=$(cat ~/.hms-mirror/hms-mirror.pid 2>/dev/null)
  [ -n "$PID" ] && jstat -gcutil $PID 5 3

} > /tmp/hms-mirror-perf-$(date +%Y%m%d-%H%M).txt

docs.title: RocksDB Diagnostics
docs.text: RocksDB is the embedded key-value database that stores all HMS-Mirror state. Problems here directly prevent the application from functioning.

docs.title: RocksDB Location
docs.text:
# Default location
~/.hms-mirror/data/

# If working directory overridden
${HMS_MIRROR_WORKING_DIR}/data/

docs.title: Signs of RocksDB Problems
docs.text: Log Pattern Severity RocksDB: Could not acquire lock Low — another instance running or stale lock RocksDB: Corruption detected High — data integrity issue RocksDB: IO error Medium — filesystem or disk issue RocksDB: No space left on device Medium — disk full Application starts but data is missing Medium — column family may be missing

docs.title: Quick Health Check
docs.text:
# Check if RocksDB directory exists and has content
ls -la ~/.hms-mirror/data/
du -sh ~/.hms-mirror/data/

# Key files that should exist
ls ~/.hms-mirror/data/CURRENT    # RocksDB manifest pointer
ls ~/.hms-mirror/data/MANIFEST-* # Change log
ls ~/.hms-mirror/data/OPTIONS-*  # RocksDB options
ls ~/.hms-mirror/data/LOCK       # Exclusive lock file (exists only while running)

docs.title: Stale Lock File
docs.text:
Symptom: Server reports "Could not acquire lock" even though no other instance is running.

Cause: Previous instance crashed without releasing the lock file.

Resolution:

# 1. Confirm no HMS-Mirror processes are running
ps aux | grep hmsm-server

# 2. Only if NO process is running — remove the lock
rm -f ~/.hms-mirror/data/LOCK

# 3. Restart the server
/path/to/hmsm-server start

Do Not Remove Lock While Server is Running

Only remove the LOCK file if you have confirmed the server process is NOT running. Removing it while running will corrupt the database.

docs.title: Disk Space Recovery
docs.text:
RocksDB writes compaction files that can accumulate. If disk is full:

# Check total usage
du -sh ~/.hms-mirror/data/

# Check overall disk
df -h ~/.hms-mirror/

# Check for large SST files (RocksDB data files)
find ~/.hms-mirror/data/ -name "*.sst" -size +100M | sort -k5 -rn

# Log files in the data directory can also be deleted safely (after stopping server)
# These are RocksDB WAL (Write-Ahead Log) files ending in .log
find ~/.hms-mirror/data/ -name "*.log" -not -name "hms-mirror*"

After clearing space, start the server and let RocksDB compact on its own.

docs.title: Backup Before Any Recovery
docs.text:
Always backup before attempting recovery:

# Stop the server first
/path/to/hmsm-server stop

# Create timestamped backup
cp -r ~/.hms-mirror/data/ ~/.hms-mirror/data.backup.$(date +%Y%m%d-%H%M)/

# Verify backup
ls -la ~/.hms-mirror/data.backup.$(date +%Y%m%d-%H%M)/

docs.title: Option 1: Restart (Fixes Many Transient Issues)
docs.text:
/path/to/hmsm-server stop
sleep 5
/path/to/hmsm-server start

RocksDB performs integrity checks on startup and can often self-repair minor issues.

docs.title: Option 2: Database Reset (Last Resort)
docs.text:
DATA LOSS

This procedure permanently deletes all user accounts, connections, configurations, jobs, and reports. Only use if all other options are exhausted and the customer accepts the consequences.

  1. Stop the server
  2. Export any recoverable data via the UI (connections, configs, datasets) if the server can still start
  3. Move the data directory:
/path/to/hmsm-server stop
mv ~/.hms-mirror/data/ ~/.hms-mirror/data.broken.$(date +%Y%m%d-%H%M)/
  1. Start the server — a fresh RocksDB will be created:
/path/to/hmsm-server start
  1. Re-create the initial admin account (see first-run-setup)
  2. Re-import any exported configurations

docs.title: Column Family Recovery
docs.text:
HMS-Mirror uses RocksDB column families to separate data by type (users, connections, jobs, etc.). If one column family is corrupted but others are intact, partial data recovery may be possible. This requires Engineering involvement — escalate with:

  • Full RocksDB data directory backup
  • List of column families affected
  • Error messages from the log

docs.title: RocksDB Metrics via API
docs.text:
If the server is running, you can query RocksDB status:

TOKEN=$(curl -s -X POST http://localhost:8090/hms-mirror/api/v1/auth/login \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"usernameOrEmail":"admin","password":"adminpass"}' | python3 -c "import sys,json; print(json.load(sys.stdin)['accessToken'])")

curl -s http://localhost:8090/hms-mirror/api/v1/status/rocksdb \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" | python3 -m json.tool

docs.title: Security Incidents
docs.text: This page covers response procedures for suspected security incidents involving HMS-Mirror.

docs.title: Types of Security Incidents
docs.text: Incident Type Severity Response Unauthorized admin access Critical Immediate — change credentials, audit, investigate Account brute-force in progress High Rate limiter should be active; verify and escalate Suspected credential leak High Rotate credentials immediately Unauthorized job execution High Suspend the account, audit job history JWT token abuse Medium Revoke all sessions (rotate JWT secret) Privilege escalation (user → admin) Critical Audit user records, investigate

docs.title: Step 1: Contain
docs.text:
If unauthorized access is confirmed or strongly suspected:

# Stop the server immediately to prevent further access
/path/to/hmsm-server stop

Warning

Stopping the server severs all active sessions. Notify the customer before doing this unless active data exfiltration is in progress.

docs.title: Step 2: Preserve Evidence
docs.text:
Before making any changes, capture the current state:

# Capture full log
cp ~/.hms-mirror/logs/hms-mirror.log /tmp/incident-$(date +%Y%m%d-%H%M)-audit.log

# Capture log with authentication events
grep -E "(login|auth|jwt|token|Admin|USER|ADMIN)" ~/.hms-mirror/logs/hms-mirror.log \
  > /tmp/incident-auth-events.log

docs.title: Step 3: Identify the Compromise Vector
docs.text:
Look for:

# Failed then successful logins (brute force succeeded?)
grep -E "Authentication failed|logged in successfully" ~/.hms-mirror/logs/hms-mirror.log | \
  grep -B 5 "logged in" | head -50

# Unusual IPs (if IP logging is enabled in a load balancer / proxy in front)
grep "logged in successfully" ~/.hms-mirror/logs/hms-mirror.log

# Admin operations performed
grep -E "ADMIN|createUser|deleteUser|setAdmin" ~/.hms-mirror/logs/hms-mirror.log | tail -50

# Jobs executed
grep "Starting job\|Job started" ~/.hms-mirror/logs/hms-mirror.log | tail -20

docs.title: Revoking All Active Sessions
docs.text:
JWT tokens cannot be individually revoked (they are stateless). The only way to revoke ALL active sessions is to rotate the JWT secret:

# Stop the server
/path/to/hmsm-server stop

# In web.properties, change or remove the JWT secret
# If removed, a new random secret is generated on next start
# All existing tokens become invalid

# Edit the secret
vi ~/.hms-mirror/settings/web.properties
# Change: hms-mirror.jwt.secret=<new-long-random-value>

# Restart
/path/to/hmsm-server start

After this, all users (including legitimate ones) must log in again.

docs.title: Account Lockout Investigation
docs.text: If an account is reporting being "unexpectedly locked":

docs.title: Verify Current Lockout State via API
docs.text:
# As admin, check user details
TOKEN=$(curl -s -X POST http://localhost:8090/hms-mirror/api/v1/auth/login \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"usernameOrEmail":"admin","password":"adminpass"}' | \
  python3 -c "import sys,json; print(json.load(sys.stdin)['accessToken'])")

# Get user list with lockout status
curl -s http://localhost:8090/hms-mirror/api/v1/users \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" | python3 -m json.tool

docs.title: Find Failed Login Events for a User
docs.text:
grep "Authentication failed\|Failed login" ~/.hms-mirror/logs/hms-mirror.log | \
  grep "<username>" | tail -20

docs.title: Unlock via API
docs.text:
# PUT /api/v1/users/{userId}/unlock
curl -X PUT http://localhost:8090/hms-mirror/api/v1/users/<userId>/unlock \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN"

docs.title: Privilege Escalation Audit
docs.text:
If you suspect a user gained admin privileges they shouldn't have:

# Check current admin users via API
curl -s http://localhost:8090/hms-mirror/api/v1/users \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" | \
  python3 -c "import sys,json; users=json.load(sys.stdin); [print(u['username'], u.get('roles','')) for u in users if 'ADMIN' in str(u.get('roles',''))]"

# Check log for admin-granting events
grep -i "admin\|ROLE_ADMIN\|setAdmin" ~/.hms-mirror/logs/hms-mirror.log | tail -30

docs.title: Post-Incident
docs.text:
After the incident is contained and investigated:

  1. Change all credentials — admin password, any configured service account passwords
  2. Rotate the JWT secret — invalidate all sessions
  3. Review user accounts — remove accounts that shouldn't exist
  4. Enable debug logging temporarily — to monitor for repeat attempts
  5. Document — what happened, timeline, remediation taken
  6. Escalate to Engineering if there is evidence of a software vulnerability (not just credential misuse)

docs.title: TLS/SSL Deep Dive
docs.text: This page covers HTTPS configuration, certificate issues, and trust chain debugging for HMS-Mirror.

docs.title: TLS Architecture
docs.text:
HMS-Mirror has two distinct TLS contexts:

Context Purpose Server TLS HTTPS for the HMS-Mirror web interface (inbound) Client TLS Trust store for connecting to Hive/HDFS clusters with TLS (outbound)

Both can have problems independently.

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
TLS is configured in ~/.hms-mirror/settings/tls.properties:

server.ssl.enabled=true
server.ssl.key-store=~/.hms-mirror/certificates/hms-mirror.p12
server.ssl.key-store-password=changeit
server.ssl.key-store-type=PKCS12
server.ssl.key-alias=hms-mirror

And optionally in web.properties: 

server.port=8443

docs.title: Verify TLS Configuration
docs.text:
# Test HTTPS connectivity
curl -vk https://localhost:8443/hms-mirror/api/v1/system/health

# View the certificate being served
openssl s_client -connect localhost:8443 -servername localhost < /dev/null 2>&1 | \
  openssl x509 -noout -text -in /dev/stdin

# Check certificate expiry
openssl s_client -connect localhost:8443 < /dev/null 2>&1 | \
  openssl x509 -noout -dates

docs.title: Common TLS Startup Errors
docs.text: Error Cause Fix java.io.FileNotFoundException: keystore Keystore file path wrong Fix path in tls.properties UnrecoverableKeyException Wrong keystore password Fix password in tls.properties java.security.KeyStoreException: PKCS12 Keystore type mismatch Ensure type=PKCS12 javax.net.ssl.SSLHandshakeException Client doesn't trust the cert Customer browser needs to trust the CA

docs.title: Creating a Self-Signed Certificate (Development)
docs.text:
# Generate a self-signed certificate (1 year validity)
keytool -genkeypair \
  -alias hms-mirror \
  -keyalg RSA \
  -keysize 2048 \
  -storetype PKCS12 \
  -keystore ~/.hms-mirror/certificates/hms-mirror.p12 \
  -validity 365 \
  -storepass changeit \
  -dname "CN=hms-mirror.example.com, OU=Support, O=Cloudera, L=Santa Clara, ST=CA, C=US"

docs.title: Client TLS (Connecting to Secured Hive/Knox)
docs.text: When HMS-Mirror connects to Hive clusters with TLS enabled, it needs to trust the cluster's certificate.

docs.title: Adding a Trusted Certificate
docs.text:
Option 1: Add to the JVM trust store

# Find Java installation
JAVA_HOME=$(dirname $(dirname $(readlink -f $(which java))))

# Import the cluster CA certificate
keytool -importcert \
  -alias source-cluster-ca \
  -file /path/to/cluster-ca.crt \
  -keystore $JAVA_HOME/lib/security/cacerts \
  -storepass changeit \
  -noprompt

Option 2: Create a custom trust store for HMS-Mirror

# Create trust store with cluster CA
keytool -importcert \
  -alias source-cluster-ca \
  -file /path/to/cluster-ca.crt \
  -keystore ~/.hms-mirror/certificates/truststore.jks \
  -storepass changeit \
  -noprompt

# Configure HMS-Mirror to use it (in web.properties or startup args)
-Djavax.net.ssl.trustStore=~/.hms-mirror/certificates/truststore.jks
-Djavax.net.ssl.trustStorePassword=changeit

docs.title: JDBC URI with SSL
docs.text:
For HiveServer2 with TLS: 

jdbc:hive2://host:10000/default;ssl=true;sslTrustStore=/path/to/truststore.jks;trustStorePassword=changeit

docs.title: Certificate Expiry
docs.text:
Certificates have expiry dates. When they expire, connections suddenly start failing with SSL errors.

Check all certificates HMS-Mirror depends on:

# Check the server certificate
openssl s_client -connect localhost:8443 < /dev/null 2>&1 | \
  openssl x509 -noout -dates

# Check a remote cluster certificate
openssl s_client -connect hiveserver2.example.com:10000 < /dev/null 2>&1 | \
  openssl x509 -noout -dates

# Find certificates in the working directory
find ~/.hms-mirror/certificates/ -name "*.crt" -o -name "*.pem" | \
  xargs -I{} openssl x509 -noout -dates -in {}

docs.title: Knox SSL Troubleshooting
docs.text:
Knox uses mutual TLS in some configurations. Common issues:

Error: PKIX path building failed: unable to find valid certification path to requested target

This means HMS-Mirror doesn't trust the Knox certificate. Solutions: 1. Import the Knox CA certificate into HMS-Mirror's trust store (see above) 2. For development: use -k in curl tests, but do NOT use ssl=false in production JDBC URIs

docs.title: Collecting TLS Diagnostic Info for Escalation
docs.text:
# Certificate chain from the server
openssl s_client -connect localhost:8443 -showcerts < /dev/null 2>&1

# SSL/TLS handshake debug (verbose, generates a lot of output)
java -Djavax.net.debug=ssl:handshake \
  -jar hmsm-server-*.jar 2>&1 | \
  grep -E "ServerHello|Certificate|Finished|Exception" | head -50

docs.title: Quick Reference
docs.text: Fast answers for support staff. All tiers.

# Last 100 lines of log

  python3 -c "import sys,json; print(json.load(sys.stdin)['accessToken'])")

docs.title: Environment Variables
docs.text: HMS-Mirror reads these environment variables at startup. They take precedence over web.properties.

docs.title: Core Variables
docs.text: Variable Default Purpose HMS_MIRROR_WORKING_DIR ~/.hms-mirror Base directory for all application data HMS_MIRROR_SERVER_PORT 8090 HTTP port HMS_MIRROR_LOG_LEVEL INFO Application log level (DEBUG, INFO, WARN, ERROR) HMS_MIRROR_LOG_DIR ${working-dir}/logs Log file directory

docs.title: Authentication Variables
docs.text: Variable Default Purpose HMS_MIRROR_AUTH_TYPE local Authentication type (local, ldap, oidc) HMS_MIRROR_JWT_SECRET (random) JWT signing secret — set for persistent sessions HMS_MIRROR_JWT_ACCESS_TTL 900 Access token lifetime in seconds (default: 15 min) HMS_MIRROR_JWT_REFRESH_TTL 604800 Refresh token lifetime in seconds (default: 7 days)

docs.title: Security Variables
docs.text: Variable Default Purpose HMS_MIRROR_KEYSTORE_PATH ${working-dir}/security/hms-mirror.p12 Encryption keystore path HMS_MIRROR_KEYSTORE_PASSWORD (from tls.properties) Keystore password HMS_MIRROR_TLS_ENABLED false Enable HTTPS HMS_MIRROR_TLS_KEYSTORE (from tls.properties) TLS keystore path HMS_MIRROR_TLS_KEYSTORE_PASSWORD (from tls.properties) TLS keystore password

docs.title: Feature Flags
docs.text: Variable Default Purpose HMS_MIRROR_LLM_ENABLED false Enable LLM/Ollama integration HMS_MIRROR_PLANNER_ENABLED false Enable AI migration planner HMS_MIRROR_TRINO_ENABLED false Enable Trino/Presto features

docs.title: JVM and Performance
docs.text: Variable Default Purpose JVM_OPTS -Xmx2g -Xms1g JVM memory settings (set before starting) HMS_MIRROR_ROCKSDB_PATH ${working-dir}/data RocksDB data directory

docs.title: Logging (JVM System Properties)
docs.text:
These are JVM system properties (-D flags), not environment variables. Set before starting the JVM:

Property Default Purpose -DLOGGING_THRESHOLD INFO Logback early-init threshold -DLOGGING_FILE_PREFIX hms-mirror Log filename prefix -DLOGGING_DIR ${working-dir}/logs Log directory for early-init

docs.title: How Variables Are Resolved
docs.text:
Priority order (highest first):

1. Environment variables (HMS_MIRROR_*)
2. ~/.hms-mirror/settings/web.properties
3. ~/.hms-mirror/settings/tls.properties
4. Spring Boot defaults
5. Built-in application defaults

docs.title: Example: Setting Variables Before Starting
docs.text:
export HMS_MIRROR_WORKING_DIR=/mnt/data/hms-mirror
export HMS_MIRROR_JWT_SECRET="$(openssl rand -base64 64)"
export HMS_MIRROR_AUTH_TYPE=ldap
export JVM_OPTS="-Xmx8g -Xms4g"

/path/to/hmsm-server start

docs.title: Cloudera Manager Variable Mapping
docs.text:
When deployed via CM, variables are set in the CM service configuration. CM parameter names typically map to environment variables with the HMS_MIRROR_ prefix stripped:

CM Parameter Environment Variable Server Port HMS_MIRROR_SERVER_PORT JWT Secret HMS_MIRROR_JWT_SECRET Auth Type HMS_MIRROR_AUTH_TYPE Log Level HMS_MIRROR_LOG_LEVEL

docs.title: Error Message Catalog
docs.text: When a customer reports an error, find the matching entry here to identify cause and next steps.

docs.title: Server / Startup Errors
docs.text: Error Message Cause Action Address already in use: 8090 Port 8090 in use by another process Check with lsof -i :8090; stop other process or change port Cannot open RocksDB — resource temporarily unavailable Another HMS-Mirror instance running, or stale LOCK file Verify no other instance; remove ~/.hms-mirror/data/LOCK if confirmed RocksDB: Corruption detected Database integrity failure L3: backup + recovery Keystore not found at path Security keystore file missing Verify ~/.hms-mirror/security/hms-mirror.p12 exists Failed to load keystore — UnrecoverableKeyException Wrong keystore password in web.properties Fix hms-mirror.keystore.password UnsupportedClassVersionError Wrong Java version Upgrade to Java 17+ Could not find or load main class Corrupted JAR or wrong startup command Re-download/rebuild the JAR Port 8090 was already in use Same as Address already in use Same resolution OutOfMemoryError at startup Heap too small Increase JVM heap with -Xmx

docs.title: Authentication Errors
docs.text: Error Message Cause Action Invalid credentials Wrong username or password Verify credentials; reset if needed Account is locked 5+ failed login attempts Admin unlocks via UI or API Too many login attempts (HTTP 429) Rate limit hit Wait 1 minute JWT token has expired Access token older than 15 minutes Use refresh token or log in again JWT signature does not match JWT secret changed since token issued Log in again Unauthorized (HTTP 401) No token provided Add Authorization: Bearer <token> header Forbidden (HTTP 403) Token valid but role insufficient User lacks required role (ADMIN vs USER) Authentication failed for user: <name> (in log) Credential verification failed Check password, LDAP connectivity LDAP connection refused LDAP server not reachable Check LDAP URL configuration

docs.title: Connection / JDBC Errors
docs.text: Error Message Cause Action Connection refused Service not running on target host/port Verify HiveServer2 is running No suitable driver found for jdbc:hive2:// Missing JDBC driver JAR Place Hive JDBC JAR in ~/.hms-mirror/aux_libs/ GSS initiate failed No valid Kerberos TGT Run kinit Clock skew too great Time difference > 5 min with KDC Sync NTP on HMS-Mirror host KDC unreachable Cannot reach KDC on port 88 Check network/firewall to KDC Server not found in Kerberos database Wrong principal in JDBC URI Verify ;principal= in JDBC URI SSL handshake failed Certificate trust issue Import cluster CA into trust store PKIX path building failed Same as SSL handshake Import CA cert Access denied for user Missing Hive/HDFS permissions Grant permissions to migration user HTTP 401 Unauthorized (Knox) Wrong Knox credentials Verify Knox username/password HTTP 403 Forbidden (Knox) Knox authorization denied Check Knox topology permissions

docs.title: Migration / Job Errors
docs.text: Error Message Cause Action Table already exists Target table exists from previous run Enable transfer.overwrite or drop table Database does not exist Target database not created Create database on target cluster Cannot export transactional table ACID table in EXPORT strategy Enable acid.downgradeAcid: true OutOfMemoryError during job Table too large for SQL strategy Switch to EXPORT_IMPORT strategy Query timed out Long-running INSERT Switch strategy or increase transfer.queryTimeout Too many dynamic partitions Partition limit on target Set hive.exec.max.dynamic.partitions override Dynamic partition strict mode Target Hive rejects dynamic partitions Set hive.exec.dynamic.partition.mode=nonstrict Permission denied on export path Can't write to HDFS staging Grant write permission on staging path No space left on device (EXPORT) Insufficient staging disk space Use different staging path Type mismatch / Type conversion Incompatible column types Review DDL at source vs target

docs.title: RocksDB Errors
docs.text: Error Message Cause Action Cannot open database Lock held or corruption L3: Check lock file, then escalate IO error Filesystem or disk issue Check disk health and space No space left on device RocksDB data dir full Free disk space Corruption detected Database integrity failure L3: Backup and recovery Permission denied: rocksdb Wrong ownership/permissions chown -R $USER ~/.hms-mirror/data/

docs.title: HTTP Error Codes
docs.text: Code Meaning Common Causes 400 Bad Request Malformed JSON, missing required fields 401 Unauthorized No token, expired token 403 Forbidden Token valid, role insufficient 404 Not Found Wrong URL (missing /hms-mirror prefix), resource deleted 409 Conflict Duplicate resource (username, connection name) 429 Too Many Requests Login rate limit 500 Internal Server Error Unhandled exception — check server log 503 Service Unavailable Server starting up or overloaded

docs.title: Who Handles What
docs.text: Issue Type L1 L2 L3 Engineering Server not starting ✓ Wrong URL / can't access UI ✓ Account locked / login issues ✓ First-run setup guide ✓ Missing JDBC driver ✓ Port conflict ✓ Log analysis Triage only ✓ JDBC connection debugging Collect info ✓ Kerberos basic (kinit) ✓ Kerberos deep (KDC, keytab) ✓ Migration failure analysis Collect info ✓ Configuration review ✓ Report interpretation ✓ TLS/certificate issues ✓ basic ✓ deep LDAP integration ✓ Performance / slow jobs Collect info ✓ initial ✓ deep OOM / JVM heap analysis ✓ initial ✓ deep RocksDB diagnostics Alert L3 ✓ RocksDB corruption/recovery ✓ Security incidents Alert L3 Alert L3 ✓ JWT secret rotation ✓ Keystore recovery ✓ API-level debugging ✓ basic ✓ deep Code bug investigation Collect info Collect info ✓ Security vulnerability Alert Engineering Alert Engineering ✓ (private)

docs.title: Escalation Response Times (SLA Guidelines)
docs.text: Priority L1 → L2 L2 → L3 L3 → Engineering P1 (Critical) Immediately Within 1 hour Within 2 hours P2 (High) Within 2 hours Within 4 hours Next business day P3 (Medium) Within 1 business day Within 2 business days Within 1 week P4 (Low) Within 2 business days Within 1 week Backlog

docs.title: Priority Definitions
docs.text: Priority Criteria P1 — Critical Production blocked, no workaround, customer data at risk P2 — High Major functionality impaired, workaround exists P3 — Medium Some functionality impaired, workaround available P4 — Low Minor issue, cosmetic, or question

docs.title: Escalation Contacts
docs.text: Tier Contact Method L2 Support ticket + Slack #hms-mirror-l2-support L3 Support ticket (tagged L3) + Slack #hms-mirror-l3-support Engineering Jira bug ticket or Slack #hms-mirror-engineering Security incidents Security team DM — do NOT use public channels

docs.title: Skip-Level Escalation
docs.text:
It is acceptable to skip L2 and go directly to L3 for:

  • RocksDB corruption suspected
  • Security incidents
  • Customer data loss risk
  • Production outage with no obvious cause

Always communicate when skip-level escalation occurs so L2 is kept in the loop.

docs.title: Network Ports
docs.text: Port Default Purpose Configurable 8090 HTTP web UI + API server.port in web.properties 8443 HTTPS (if TLS enabled) server.port + server.ssl.enabled=true

docs.title: URL Paths
docs.text:
URL Notes http://host:8090/hms-mirror Web UI home http://host:8090/hms-mirror/api/v1/ REST API base http://host:8090/hms-mirror/api/v1/system/health Health check (no auth) http://host:8090/hms-mirror/api/v1/auth/login Login (no auth) http://host:8090/hms-mirror/docs/ Embedded documentation

Warning

All URLs are under /hms-mirror. Requests to / return 404. This is expected.

docs.title: Working Directory Structure
docs.text:
Default: ~/.hms-mirror/ (override via HMS_MIRROR_WORKING_DIR)

~/.hms-mirror/
├── data/                   ← RocksDB embedded database
│   ├── CURRENT             ← RocksDB manifest pointer
│   ├── MANIFEST-*          ← Change log
│   ├── OPTIONS-*           ← RocksDB options
│   ├── LOCK                ← Exclusive lock (exists only while running)
│   └── *.sst               ← Data files
├── logs/
│   ├── hms-mirror.log      ← Current log (100MB, then rotated)
│   └── hms-mirror.*.log.gz ← Rotated/compressed logs (30 days)
├── security/
│   └── hms-mirror.p12      ← Encryption keystore (PKCS12)
├── certificates/
│   └── *.p12 / *.jks       ← TLS certificates (if TLS enabled)
├── settings/
│   ├── web.properties      ← Application configuration overrides
│   └── tls.properties      ← TLS configuration
├── drivers/                ← JDBC driver JARs
├── aux_libs/               ← Additional JARs (JDBC drivers, etc.)
├── reports/
│   └── <job-id>/           ← Migration output reports
│       ├── conversion-result.yaml
│       ├── report.html
│       ├── left-execute.sql
│       └── right-execute.sql
├── hadoop-conf/            ← Hadoop configuration files (krb5, core-site, etc.)
├── llms/                   ← Embedded Ollama models (if LLM feature enabled)
└── hms-mirror.pid          ← PID file (exists while running)

docs.title: Configuration Files
docs.text: File Purpose ~/.hms-mirror/settings/web.properties Port, auth type, JWT secret, feature flags ~/.hms-mirror/settings/tls.properties TLS keystore path and password ~/.hms-mirror/hadoop-conf/core-site.xml Hadoop core configuration (for HDFS access) ~/.hms-mirror/hadoop-conf/hdfs-site.xml HDFS configuration (NameNode addresses) /etc/krb5.conf Kerberos client configuration (system-wide)

docs.title: Log Files
docs.text: File Contents Rotation ~/.hms-mirror/logs/hms-mirror.log Current log 100MB trigger ~/.hms-mirror/logs/hms-mirror.YYYY-MM-DD.N.log.gz Rotated logs Kept 30 days, max 1GB total

docs.title: Key API Endpoints (All under /hms-mirror/api/v1/)
docs.text: Method Path Auth Purpose POST /auth/login No Authenticate, get tokens POST /auth/refresh No Refresh access token POST /auth/logout Yes Logout (client-side token discard) GET /system/health No Health check GET /status Admin Full system status GET /status/jvm Admin JVM memory/thread status GET /status/rocksdb Admin RocksDB status GET /users Admin List users POST /users Admin Create user PUT /users/{id}/unlock Admin Unlock locked account PUT /users/{id}/admin-reset-password Admin Reset user password GET /connections User List connections POST /connections/{id}/test User Test connection GET /jobs User List jobs POST /jobs/{id}/run User Run a job
